coredata.dc:title: Glucosaminic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots for sensitive detection of lactose in living cells and real food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Chaoqun
Liu, Bin
Fang, Yuying
Zhou, Ruitao
Qian, Liangliang
Tang, Shipeng
Ou, Shengju
Cheng, Rumei
coredata.dc:description: The design of a nonenzyme sensor for lactose imaging in living cells is still a challenge nowadays. Enlightened by the saccharide–saccharide recognition at the cell surface, we prepared a graphene quantum dots–glucosaminic acid (GQDs–GA)-based fluorescent sensor for detection of lactose in living cells with high selectivity and sensitivity. GA acts as a receptor for lactose, while GQDs acts as a fluorescence signal transducer. Upon lactose addition, the fluorescence peaks at 315 and 429 nm were significantly enhanced owing to the blocking of photoinduced electron transfer. The interaction of the GQDs–GA sensor with lactose was mainly governed by the amide•••OH and OH•••π hydrogen bonding, supported by 1H NMR and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. A good linear relationship with the concentration of lactose was obtained over the range of 9.9 − 142.9 μM with a detection limit of 0.35 μM. The proposed sensor was used to detect lactose in real samples (tap water, milk, and honey) with a recovery range of 100.16–103.32%. More importantly, the GQDs–GA sensor can be used to track lactose in living cells. This work provides new insights into development of nonenzyme-based biosensor for single saccharide.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots–glucosaminic acid
Lactose
Fluorescent sensor
Molecular model
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging of lactose in vitro
Detection of lactose in real samples
Sensing behavior of GQDs-GA to lactose
Compared with recently reported fluorescent sensor for lactose detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21405115 ), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology ( Y20210081 ), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology , Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University ( J02-20210202 ) are acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman spectra of (i) GQDs and (ii) GQDs-GA; (b) FT-IR spectra of (i) GA, (ii) GQDs, (iii) GQDs-GA;(c) XRD spectra of (i) GQDs and (ii) GQDs-GA; (d) UV–vis spectra of GQDs-GA (5 mg/L) by addition of different concentrations of lactose (0–150 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-GA (5 mg/L) upon addition of different concentrations lactose (0–142.9 μM) at emission peak 315 nm and emission peak 429 nm in aqueous solution; (b) The calibration plot of linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of lactose at emission peak 315 nm; (c) The calibration curve of relative fluorescence intensity as a function of lactose concentration at 429 nm.
1H NMR spectra of (i) free lactose, (ii) GQDs-GA, and (iii) GQDs-GA-lactose complex in D2O.

Optimized structures of the GQDs-GA/lactose complexes: (a) lactose adsorbed to the epoxy-containing flat surface; (b) lactose adsorbed to the pure carbon-carbon bonds flat surface. (Gray: carbon; red: oxygen; blue: nitrogen; white: hydrogen).

Fluorescence response of GQD-GA sensing system to various biomolecules and selectivity of GQD-GA sensing system for lactose in the presence of interferent in aqueous solution. 1, lactose (only)；2, sucrose；3, maltose; 4, fructose; 5, glucosamine; 6, glucose; 7, galactose; 8, leucine; 9, threonine; 10, methionine; 11, glutamate; 12, glycin; 13, Na+; 14, Mg2+; 15, K+; 16, Ca2+; 17, Al3+. (red column: GQDs-GA + interferent; green column: GQDs-GA + interferent + lactose). [GQDs-GA]= 5 mg/L, [lactose]= 140 μM, [interfere]= 700 μM.
Confocal fluorescence images of ARPE-19 cells. (i) Cells incubated with GQDs-GA as control group; followed by addition of (ii) 20 μM and (iii) 50 μM lactose, respectively. Scale bar: 20 µm.

Schematic representation of the sensing mechanism of GQDs–GA as a fluorescent turn-on sensor for the detection of lactose. Star symbol means lactose and arrow means direction of electrons transfer. Abbreviation: glucosaminic acid ( GA); photoinduced electron transfer (PET).
Comparison of the other reported fluorescent sensor for determination of lactose.

row.entry: bovine serum albumin stabilized Au nanoclusters (AuNCs), β-galactosidase (β-Gal) and glucose oxidase (GOx)

head.ce:title: Glucosaminic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots for sensitive detection of lactose in living cells and real food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials for Nano-Bio Applications, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, 270 Xueyuan Xi Road, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Zheda Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Science Park, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310013, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: C. Zhou, B. Liu, and Y. Fang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of a nonenzyme sensor for lactose imaging in living cells is still a challenge nowadays. Enlightened by the saccharide–saccharide recognition at the cell surface, we prepared a graphene quantum dots–glucosaminic acid (GQDs–GA)-based fluorescent sensor for detection of lactose in living cells with high selectivity and sensitivity. GA acts as a receptor for lactose, while GQDs acts as a fluorescence signal transducer. Upon lactose addition, the fluorescence peaks at 315 and 429 nm were significantly enhanced owing to the blocking of photoinduced electron transfer. The interaction of the GQDs–GA sensor with lactose was mainly governed by the amide•••OH and OH•••π hydrogen bonding, supported by 1H NMR and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. A good linear relationship with the concentration of lactose was obtained over the range of 9.9 − 142.9 μM with a detection limit of 0.35 μM. The proposed sensor was used to detect lactose in real samples (tap water, milk, and honey) with a recovery range of 100.16–103.32%. More importantly, the GQDs–GA sensor can be used to track lactose in living cells. This work provides new insights into development of nonenzyme-based biosensor for single saccharide.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactose is a disaccharide formed from glucose and galactose, and plays an important role in human nutrition [1]. Abnormal lactose concentration in humans may imply a health problem [2]. Normally, the concentrations of lactose in blood are 110–194 μmol/L. It has been reported that high lactose concentration in the blood indicates gastrointestinal cancer [3]. Tumor-derived cytokines (such as IL-6 and TNF-α) act as signals resulting in the disruption of metabolism of lactose. Therefore, the detection of lactose can assist in clinical diagnosis. Recently, lactose detection has attracted considerable attention [4,5].

Although chromatography and mass spectrometry have been used to detect lactose [6–8], they require professional operators and complex sample processing. Also, these methods are not suitable for the tracking intracellular lactose in vivo. The development of a rapid and simple method for the detection of intracellular lactose is highly desired. Fluorescent detection methods have been increasingly studied because they are simple, rapid, sensitive, and suitable for intracellular detection [9]. However, there are many disaccharides (i.e., sucrose and maltose) having a glucose unit and multiple hydroxyls like lactose. The design of a fluorescence sensor for lactose remains a great challenge [10]. So far, a few fluorescence lactose sensors were reported. A cobalt metal–organic framework (MOF) sensitively detected lactose through potential supramolecular interactions (suppressing a photoinduced electron transfer course) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 30 mM [11]. β-Galactosidase-functionalized zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 (ZIF-8) composite can sense lactose with an LOD of 0.14 mM [12]. A complex sensor fabricated from Au nanoclusters, β-galactosidase, glucose oxidase, and ZIF-8 had an LOD of 15 mM [13]. The use of glucose oxidase was to eliminate the interference from glucose. An LOD of 11.36 μM was achieved by lens culinaris β-galactosidase-modified nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots [14]. The mechanism of these sensors containing β-galactosidase to recognize lactose is mainly based on the enzyme catalysis reactions, accompanied by changes in fluorescence intensity.

However, most of these sensors suffered from irreversible binding, affected by glucose and the unstable modification of enzymes. In addition, they were not suitable for intracellular lactose imaging. A notable finding of the above studies was that the application of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorophores increased sensor sensitivity, reducing the LOD. The high quantum yield of graphene-based nanomaterials can produce a strong fluorescence intensity, increasing sensitivity toward targeted analytes with a low LOD and wide dynamic ranges [15–17]. Thus, it is possible to use GQDs as a fluorophore by linking with a suitable receptor for lactose recognition.

The saccharide–saccharide interaction is ubiquitous at the cell surface for host–guest molecular recognition [18,19]. Their high specificity is controlled by multiple hydrogen bonds among saccharides [20,21]. By adopting this strategy, the design of a fluorescence sensor for saccharide detection would be more flexible than using enzymes since the interaction force based on hydrogen bonding is reversible [22]. Intramolecular hydrogen bonding is formed between the 3-OH of lactose and the 7-OH of sialyl, and the amine group stabilizes the conformation [23,24]. The electrochemical detection of lactose using Co–hemin MOF/chitosan and cellobiose dehydrogenase exhibited a higher affinity of chitosan to lactose [25]. The presence of lactose as a plasticizer for chitosan led to strong hydrogen bonding between lactose and the glucosamine unit of chitosan, although detailed interaction modes require further investigation [26]. The authors reported that the aminosaccharide strongly attracted lactose and the 2-amine group played important role. Stimulated by these findings, we prepared a fluorescence sensor using glucosaminic acid (GA) and GQDs for the selective detection of intracellular lactose ( Scheme 1). The GA is an amino acid, usually as a sweetener and flavoring agent. It is a biocompatible and nontoxic and often used in the asymmetric synthesis of pharmaceutical industry because of its chiral properties. GQDs act as a fluorophore with low cytotoxicity, good solubility, chemical inertness, and stable photoluminescence [27,28]. GA acts as a receptor for lactose recognition. The electron transfer from GA to GQDs through the amide linkage leads to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs, known as photoinduced electron transfer (PET). After binding with lactose, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs–GA sensor enhances with the increase in lactose concentration. The GQDs–GA sensor can be used to image intracellular lactose by confocal microscopy. The GQDs–GA sensor exhibits good water solubility, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and high selectivity, suggesting its potential in bioanalysis and biodetection. It also provides new insights into the design of fluorescent nanosensors for saccharide detection with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Graphite power, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), D-saccharides, and L-amino acids were purchased from Shanghai Reagents Company. The other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Deionized water was used throughout all experiments.

AFM (atomic force microscopy) image of GQDs-GA was taken using an SPI3800N microscope operating in the tapping mode. TEM (transmission electron microscopy) images were observed using a PHILIPS EM400ST microscope at an accelerating voltage of 150 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALB MK-II spectrometer. FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) spectra were performed on a PE Spectrum One spectrometer with KBr pellets. UV-Vis spectra were collected on a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded with a Renishaw 2000 equipped with an Ar+ ion laser having an excitation line of 514.5 nm and an air-cooling charge-coupled device as the detector. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ ionization time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectra were measured by the Autoflex mass spectrometer using delayed extraction and a nitrogen laser. Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was conducted on an Agilent 6545 LC/Q-TOF.

GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of graphene oxides [29]. GQDs–GA was prepared according to a previous report [30] with minor modifications as follows: 50 mg GQDs were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and EDC (1 mM) was added to it. After stirring for 0.5 h at room temperature, NHS (1 mM) was added to the solution, and GA (0.1 M) solution was titrated to the activated GQD solution. Then, the mixture was stirred for 48 h. Finally, the resulting solution was dialyzed (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) against deionized water for three days.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were conducted using a 270 Electrochemistry System (EG&G, USA). A conventional three-electrode system (a glass carbon working electrode (GCE), a Pt wire counter electrode, and an saturated calomel electrode (SCE) reference electrode) was used. GCE (4 mm diameter) was polished to a mirror finish using the bovine serum albumin (BSA) polishing kit with 0.3 µm and 0.05 µm alumina paste. The polished GCE was rinsed in distilled water. Then it was placed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 5 and a potential of + 1.8 V was applied for 300 s. Continuous scanning was performed between − 0.5 V and + 1.2 V until a steady-state current–voltage curve was obtained. The GQDs–GA/GCE was fabricated by adding 5 μL GQDs–GA (10 mg/L) on the pretreated GCE and drying in air. The potential-stripping voltammograms were recorded from − 0.4 V to + 1.0 V at a sweep rate of 50 mV/s. CV measurements were conducted in 1.0 M KCl solution with 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6/ K4[Fe(CN)6] at pH 7.5.

Theoretical calculations were achieved by the density functional theory (DFT) method to investigate the interactions between GQDs-GA and lactose. All calculations were taken using the Gaussian 16 program. DFT calculations with the B3LYP-D3 functional [31] and the 6–311 G+ (2d,2p) basis set [32] in water (polarizable continuum model (PCM) [33] with SMD-coulomb atomic radii were used to locate all the stationary points involved.

The graphene surface was modified by hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, and epoxy groups to construct a single layer GQDs model having a molecular formula of C55H18O5. A glucosaminate cooperating with a GQDs sheet via amide group produced the GQDs-GA. The used model of both the GQDs-GA and lactose is neutrally charged. The adsorption energy (Ead) is defined as.
(1) Ead=EG+L − EG − EL
where EL, EG and EG+L represent the energies of lactose, GQDs-GA and their complex, respectively [34]. L and G are lactose and GQDs-GA, respectively.

Acute retinal pigment epithelial − 19 (ARPE-19) cells were cultured as our previous paper [35]. The cell cytotoxicity of GQDs–GA was examined using the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay with different concentrations and incubation times. The cytotoxic experiments were conducted in triplicate.

Typically, lactose stock solution concentration is 0.001 M. The different volumes of lactose (0 −500 μL) was mixed with a 3 mL aqueous solution of GQDs–GA (5 mg/L) at various concentrations at room temperature. The lactose concentrations at above mixed solution is within 0 − 142.9 μM. Then, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs–GA was measured using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. Different amount of GQDs-GA (5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, and 20 mg/L) showed similar behaviors, but lower concentration of GQDs-GA is much stable and cheap. To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensor for lactose, other biomolecules and metal ions were added into the solution of GQDs–GA in a similar process. All experiments were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging of lactose in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: ARPE-19 cells were first incubated at 37 ℃ overnight. Then, the cells were divided into three groups and incubated in 24-well plates for 24 h. The first group treated with GQDs–GA (5 mg/L) was incubated for 2 h. The cells in the second and third groups, treated with GQDs–GA for 2 h, were incubated for 0.5 h with the culture solution having different concentrations of lactose (20 μM, and 50 μM). Finally, the three groups were repeatedly washed three times with PBS. The cell images were collected by confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of lactose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applications of the GQDs-GA for determination of lactose, tap water, honey, and milk were collected from the local area (Wenzhou, China). The tap water was filtered by 0.22-μm filter membranes prior to analysis. Milk and honey samples were pretreated according to a previous study [36].

The AFM characterization of GQDs exhibits a topographic height of approximately 3.0 nm, indicating that the GQDs were 4–6 graphene layers (Fig. S1a) [37]. After linking with GA, a height increase of about 0.5 nm was obtained (Fig. S1b). The TEM image of GQDs showed a discoid structure, and they were monodisperse in the aqueous solution with a size of approximately 28 nm (Fig. S1c). The GQDs-GA still maintained the uniform discoid morphology of GQDs (Fig. S1d).

The Raman spectra of GQDs and GQDs–GA displayed two prominent peaks at ∼1373 cm−1 (D band) and ∼1615 cm−1 (G band) with an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.993 and 0.977, respectively ( Fig. 1a). Compared to that of GQDs, the intensity ratio of GQDs–GA showed a lower value, indicating an increased number of sp2 clusters as well as a decrease in the number of defect sites upon the conjugation of GA with GQDs [38]. The XRD of GQDs-GA exhibited a peak at 23.6° similar to that of GQDs (Fig. 1c), revealing that the graphene structure remained intact after functionalization with GA [39]. FTIR spectra (Fig. 1b) indicated that the stretching vibrations of –OH and C–H of GQDs were located at 3397 cm−1 and 2921 cm−1, respectively. Compared with GQDs, the hydroxyl group peak in GQDs–GA shifted from 3397 cm−1 to 3429 cm−1. At the same time, the C–H peak located at 2920 cm−1 strengthened, and the amide band at 1625 cm−1 appeared. A broad peak centered at 1062 cm−1 suggested the incorporation of the C–O vibrations of GA. Both GQDs and GQDs–GA exhibited characteristic peaks at 1447 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1, which were ascribed to the bent vibration of C–H.

The XPS spectrum of GQDs showed the C1s and O1s peaks at 284.7 eV and 530 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, GQDs–GA showed an additional N1s peak at 399.75 eV, ascribed to the attachment of GA onto the edge of GQDs with a C/N atomic ratio of 11.7:1 (Fig. S2). The high-resolution C1s spectrum of GQDs revealed the presence of C–C (284.62 eV), C–OH (285.52 eV), C–O–C (288.32 eV), and O–CO (289.52 eV). Additional C–N (285.1 eV) and N–CO (288.8 eV) groups were observed in the high-resolution C1s spectrum of GQDs–GA. The peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.25 eV corresponding to C–N and N–CO functional groups were found in the high-resolution N1s spectrum. These results exhibit good agreement with the formation of GQDs–GA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing behavior of GQDs-GA to lactose
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis spectrum of GQDs-GA showed two shoulder peaks at 230 and 276 nm, which were assigned to the π-π * transition of CC at GQDs and the n–π * transition of HN–CO (related to heteroatom p-π conjugation), respectively (Fig. 1d). Upon excitation at 276 nm, the emission peaks of GQDs–GA were observed at 429 and 315 nm, which originated from the n-π * and π-π * transitions, respectively [40,41]. The peak at 315 nm was much weaker than the peak at 429 nm. Upon the addition of lactose, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs–GA at 429 nm and 315 nm increased significantly ( Fig. 2a). A good linear response was obtained between the relative fluorescence intensity and the concentration of lactose with the concentration ranging from 9.9 μM to 142.9 μM at 315 nm. The linear regression equation is F/F0 = 0.055 × Clactose + 1.89 with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (F and F0 are fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of lactose, respectively) (Fig. 2b). The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) at 429 nm as a function of lactose concentration was also observed, and the calibration plot exhibited a good linear relationship, where F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities at 429 nm in the presence and absence of lactose, respectively (Fig. 2c). The F/F0 value at 429 nm shows lactose concentration dependence in the range of 29.1 μM–142.9 μM, indicating that GQDs–GA can function as an ideal probe for the emission measurement of lactose. The calibration equation is F/F0 = 0.0018 × Clactose + 1.18 with a correlation coefficient of 0.97. The LOD was calculated to be 0.35 μM (S/N ≥ 3). Aqueous solution of GQDs-GA also gave a sharp peak at 290 nm (excitation at 276 nm), which was associated with the water Raman scattering (WRS). Such phenomenon has been reported in many fluorescent dyes solutions [42,43]. To a certain extent, WRS intensity raised with the increase of the concentrations of organic compounds and salts in water. But the relationship between WRS intensity and solute concentration was usually nonlinear in spectrofluorimetry [44]. So, the WRS in spectrofluorimetry is not used to determine lactose quantitatively. UV-Vis titration experiments showed that absorbances at 230 nm decreased while those at 276 nm raised gradually with an increase in lactose concentration in the GQDs–GA solution, indicating that lactose accelerates the interaction process (Fig. 1d). Compared with GQDs-GA, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs at 429 nm and 334 nm increased slightly with the increase of lactose content in solution (Fig. S3).

Interestingly, the increase in the emission peak at 315 nm is higher than that at 429 nm. The enhanced intensity at 429 nm is attributed to the interaction between lactose and amide, while the emission increase at 315 nm can be ascribed to the interaction between lactose and the sp2 honeycomb lattice of GQDs. It is well-known that acid oxidation during the preparation of GQDs produces oxygen functional groups (i.e., carboxylate, hydroxyl, and epoxy) on the basal plane and edges of graphene crystallites [45]. Modified by GA, the carboxyl became amide. In a typical PET sensor, the GA acts as an electron donor, while the module containing the sp2 carbon honeycomb lattice with carbonyls acts as an electron acceptor. The donor and acceptor are linked by amide conjugation. Upon optical excitation, an electron in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the fluorophore (GQDs) is excited to its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. Thereafter, the electron in the HOMO of the donor groups jumps to the HOMO of the fluorophore, causing quenching. After binding with the target molecule lactose, the interaction between lactose and amide disrupted the electron transfer from GA to the GQDs. It blocked the PET quenching process resulting in a significantly enhanced fluorescence emission. OH•••π intermolecular interactions might occur between lactose and graphene-conjugated rings, increasing the emission at 315 nm. OH•••π interactions are often observed in aromatic compounds with polyols [46,47]. Many studies concerned the peak of GQDs at about 440 nm, but the emission at 315 nm was seldom reported [48]. The increase in fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S4) from 1.194 to 1.374 also supports the fluorescence enhancement induced by the restrained PET process, implying energy transfer from the electron donor GA to the electron acceptor GQDs by employing the amide bond as a linker [49]. The findings were further confirmed by the 1H NMR spectra.

Fig. 3 shows the broad 1H NMR peaks of GQDs–GA due to strong hydrogen bonding. The chemical shifts from 6.5 ppm to 6.7 ppm suggest the protons on sp2 carbons, while two broad peaks at 2.3 ppm and 2.7 ppm are ascribed to the protons at sp3 carbons on GQDs [50]. The C–H signals on GA were located in the range of 3.0–4.7 ppm [51]. After lactose was added to the GQDs–GA solution, strong hydrogen bonding took place between GQDs–GA and lactose, inducing the broadening of the peak lines of GQDs–GA/lactose and the disappearance of the hyperfine lines observed in free lactose. The proton signals on sp2 carbons downfield shifted to 6.7–6.9 ppm, and the proton peaks of sp3 carbons on GQDs downfield shifted to 2.8 and 3.1 ppm, implying that the aromatic rings of GQDs interacted with lactose via hydrogen bonding. Note that the signal of C16–H on GQDs–GA/lactose downfield shifted from 4.3 to 4.5 ppm, forming hydrogen bonding with C7–H on lactose. The peak of C15–H also downfield shifts to 4.8 ppm. The C15–H and C16–H signal changes suggest the hydrogen bonding between lactose and amide. Although the detailed structure of the GQDs–GA/lactose complex cannot be well deduced, the 1H NMR investigation indicates that lactose binds to the amide and honeycomb lattice of GQDs–GA via hydrogen bonding, consistent with the fluorescence changes.

The quantum mechanics calculations further elucidated the interaction of lactose with GQDs-GA observed from the above investigations. The initial geometries of the GQDs-GA, and the its complexes with lactose are performed in aqueous phase using the Gaussian package [52] at ωB97XD with the standard 6–311 G+ (2d,2p) basis set. The ωB97XD functional integrating the short-range, long-range, and explicit empirical dispersion corrections is an ideal functional to investigated host-guest interactions [53]. The constructed GQDs (C55H18O5) had a molecular weight very close to the m/z of 759 found in MALDI-TOF mass spectrum (Fig. S5). According to the previous reports, the hydroxyls and carboxylates were located on the edges of as-prepared GQDs [54,55]. We considered that GQDs might have two different flat surfaces on a graphene sheet. One flat surface has the epoxy group while the other are pure carbon-carbon bonds. Values of Ead for the interaction between lactose and GQDs-GA of two initial configurations were calculated to be − 1.03 eV ( Fig. 4a) and − 1.39 eV (Fig. 4b), respectively. These suggested that interactions between GQDs-GA and lactose were physical interactions, as typically found for hydrogen bonding [56]. In model a, amide N and carboxylate O (on GA) formed hydrogen bonds with different hydroxyl O on glucopyranose and galactopyranosyl moieties, respectively. Lactose is parallel to the glucosaminate chain. But for model b, lactose is parallel to basal plane of graphene surface. The amide O and carboxylate O interacted with hydroxyl O on galactopyranosyl. These indicated that the amide (NH−CO) and carboxylate on GA played important roles in adsorption of lactose onto GQDs-GA. Interestingly, the OH•••π and CH•••π interactions were found in the range of 2.914 − 3.567 Å. The computational results confirmed that amide bond strongly interacted with lactose through hydrogen bonding, which corresponded to the results of 1H NMR. These interaction modes favored the GQDs-GA selectively sensing lactose as shown in the following studies.

As expected, the hydrogen interactions between GQDs-GA and lactose were affected by solution pHs. With the increase in solution pH from 3.5 to 7.5, the fluorescence intensity changes ΔF raised with the addition of lactose (Fig. S6). At lower pHs, a large amount of H+ attached to the surface of GQDs–GA repulsed the protonated lactose. When the pH increased above 7.5, partial hydroxyls on lactose deprotonated. It prevented the deprotonated lactose from reaching GQDs–GA resulting in reduction of ΔF. The optimal pH value was 7.5. It is worth noting that the influence of pH on fluorescence change at 315 nm is significantly higher than that at 429 nm. This suggests that the OH•••π interaction differed from the amide•••OH interaction.

Such interactions of lactose with GQDs–GA resulted in saccharide molecules on the surface of GQDs–GA. Similar to the UV–vis study, the electrochemistry is a method to characterize the molecular interaction mechanism. Electrochemistry has high sensitivity in analyte detection and it is easy to detect the adsorption mechanism of sensors to different saccharides. CV as an electrochemical method confirmed the interaction between GQDs-GA and lactose. It also evidently distinguished the interaction force between GQDs-GA and different polyhydroxy compounds. The electrochemical measurements support the covering of lactose on GQDs–GA. Fig. S7a exhibits the reversible behavior of the redox probe ferri/ferrocyanide with the anodic (Epa) and cathodic (Epc) peaks at 0.35 and 0.15 V on the bare GCE, respectively. The ΔEp (Epa–Epc) was 0.2 V in 1 M KCl solution [57]. After the treatment of the GCE with GQDs–GA, the ΔEp increased to 0.28 V with the reduction of peak currents. It indicated the lower electrochemical reversibility due to the adsorption of GQDs–GA on the GCE [58]. In the presence of 120 μM lactose, the ΔEp returned to 0.2 V, giving similar electrochemical reversibility to bare GCE. The decrease in currents suggested that lactose strongly interacted with GQDs–GA, preventing ferri/ferrocyanide species from approaching the GQDs–GA/GCE. Such CV changes were always observed in many electrochemical sensors [59]. Various saccharides had different affinities to the GQDs–GA/GCE, resulting in different changes in decreased currents on the GQDs–GA/GCE (Fig. S7b). The influence of monosaccharides was smaller than that of disaccharides. The decrease in currents in lactose solution was much larger than those examined for the other disaccharides. It showed good selectivity toward lactose. Further fluorescence response to lactose in the presence of interferences supported the electrochemical observations.

At the optimal solution pH, other biomolecules and ions were tested under identical conditions, including lactose, sucrose, maltose, fructose, glucosamine, glucose, galactose, leucine, threonine, methionine, glutamate, glycine, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, and Al3+, which did not show a turn-on fluorescence response ( Fig. 5). It was noteworthy that the fluorescence response of GQDs–GA was observed after the addition of lactose in the presence of five times more concentrated biomolecules and cations. These results evidently showed the high selectivity of GQDs–GA toward lactose, even in the presence of interferences.

The lactose significantly affected the function of cells. Red cells treated by lactose became highly permeable to cations and rapidly losing Na+ and K+ [60]. Lactose is tremendously expressed on the surface of hepatocellular carcinoma cells [61]. Thus, monitoring the lactose in cells can assist in clinical pathologic process. We tried to use the GQDs-GA to image lactose in human cells. The cell toxicity of GQDs–GA was evaluated using the CCK8 assay to reveal its potential for biological applications. Fig. S8 shows the viability of ARPE-19 cells incubated with various concentrations of GQDs–GA ranging from 25 to 150 μg/mL. The viability was above 90% at a concentration of 25 μg/mL for an incubation time of 48 h. Moreover, the viability was higher than 75% even at a concentration of 150 μg/mL. These experimental results revealed the excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity of GQDs–GA. The capability of GQDs–GA for intracellular lactose detection was investigated by confocal microscopy ( Fig. 6). Three groups of cells were incubated at 37 ℃ for 24 h under the given condition. The skeletons of ARPE-19 cells were dyed with red using rhodamine–phalloidin, as shown in Fig. 6b, e, and h. The overlay images were presented in Fig. 6c, f, and i. The first group was cultured with GQDs–GA for another 2 h. The cells treated with GQDs–GA showed the intracellular blue fluorescence signal. At the same time, the second and third groups were treated with various concentrations of lactose for another 0.5 h after incubation with GQDs–GA for 2 h. The blue fluorescence intensity of cells evidently improved compared to that in the absence of lactose. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of cells treated with GQDs–GA showed a lactose concentration dependence (Fig. 6d and g). No obvious changes were found in cell morphology in the confocal fluorescence images, indicating that GQDs–GA exhibited good biocompatibility and low toxicity and can be utilized as a nanosensor to detect intracellular lactose.

To estimate the applicability of GQDs–GA in real samples, the standard addition method was employed. Herein, the tap water, milk, and honey were chosen as real samples for the detection of lactose. The contents of lactose in tap water, milk, and honey were tested by LC-MS before performing the fluorescence method using GQDs–GA. After pretreatment, the samples were mixed with different concentrations of lactose. As can be seen from Table S1, satisfactory recoveries for lactose were obtained ranging from 100.16% to 103.32% with a relative standard deviation of 0.25–4.84% (n = 3). These experimental results suggested that the GQDs-GA can provide accurate detection of lactose in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compared with recently reported fluorescent sensor for lactose detection
ce:section.ce:para: Recent reports on fluorescent sensors for the detection of lactose were summarized in Table 1. Most of the sensors have wide linear range and can be used to measure the micro amount of lactose. Many attentions focused on β-galactosidase employed as lactose receptor [11,13]. However, the glucose or galactose often interfered the signal transduction. Although the Hydroquinone/H2O2 system on poly[2,7- (9,9-dihexyl-fuorene)]- b- cationicquaternized- poly [2-(dimethylamino) ethyl methacrylate] simultaneously detects lactose, sucrose, maltose and glucose, it still gives a low LOD of 3.08 μM for lactose. The summarized fluorescent sensors for lactose were not applied in cell imaging. In our case, the LOD of 0.35 μM is much lower than the reported LOD. Practically, the GQDs-GA in this work can be used to catch sight of lactose in living cells.

In summary, GQDs–GA was developed as a turn-on fluorescence sensor for lactose detection with high selectivity and sensitivity through a PET suppression mechanism. The GQDs–GA sensor showed two emission peaks at 315 nm and 429 nm, and the fluorescence intensity significantly enhanced after the addition of lactose. Such interactions predominantly controlled by the amide•••OH and OH•••π hydrogen bonding, and the carboxylate of GA favored to stable the geometries. They were confirmed by 1H NMR, electrochemical, and DFT studies. The F/F0 value at 315 nm as a function of lactose concentration was observed, and a good linear relationship was found in the range of 9.9–142.9 μM. The LOD was 0.35 μM. In the presence of five times more concentrated interferences, the GQDs–GA sensor detected lactose selectively. The CCK8 assay results proved the low toxicity and good biocompatibility of GQDs–GA. In addition, confocal microscopy observations confirmed that GQDs–GA can detect lactose in living cells. The GQDs–GA sensor can be applied to detect lactose in real samples (tap water, milk, and honey) with good recoveries. This study provides new insights into nanosensor development based on saccharide–saccharide recognition.

Chaoqun Zhou: Performed the experiments, analyzed data, wrote the paper. Bin Liu: Theoretical calculations, analyzed data, revised paper. Yuying Fang: Performed the experiments, Validation, Revised the paper. Ruitao Zhou: Performed the titration experiments. Liangliang Qian: Cell imagine and cytotoxicity study. Shipeng Tang: Performed the experiments, analyzed data. Shengju Ou: Validation, Formal analysis, revised the paper. Rumei Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. C. Zhou, B. Liu, and Y. Fang contributed equally. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel luminescent sensor for disaccharide detection in food: synthesis and application of a water-soluble rod-coil ionic block copolymer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wu, J. Li, M. Zhou, A novel luminescent sensor for disaccharide detection in food: Synthesis and application of a water-soluble rod-coil ionic block copolymer, Eur. Polym. J. 112 (2019) 248–254.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chaoqun Zhou studied material science at the South China University of Technology and received her master’s degree. In 2020, she became a PhD candidate in the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University. Her current research topics include construction and logic gate operation of fluorescence detection system for biological small molecule recognition using graphene quantum dots.

Bin Liu studied computer science and technology in Jiangxi Agricultural University. In his bachelor's thesis, he studied the development of online fruit mall system. He is now a graduate student in the School of Ophthalmology of Wenzhou Medical University.

Yuying Fang studied pharmaceutical engineering at Anhui University of Science and Technology. In 2021, she started her master at the Laboratory for Nanotechnology Applications at Wenzhou Medical University.

Ruitao Zhou, MD, PhD, is a clinical scientist in ophthalmology. He has been working in the School of Ophthalmology and Eye Hospital at the Wenzhou Medical University since 2015. His research focuses on fundus disease and flourescein fundus angiography.
Liangliang Qian is a graduate student at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University. His current research involves nano biosensors and logic gate construction.

Shipeng Tang is a graduate student majoring in biomedical engineering at Wenzhou Medical University. His current research topics include fabrication of amino functionalized graphene and molecular logic gates.

Shengju Ou completed his PhD in inorganic chemistry in 2006 from the Nanjing University. Thereafter, he joined the Guangxi Teacher’s University and studied the biosensors for amino acids and saccharides. Subsequently, he joined the Zhejiang University National Science Park, where he established Femtosecond Research Center. His current research interest is molecular recognition mechanisms.

Rumei Cheng studied chemistry at Tongji University and received her PhD. She has been working at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University since 2010. She focuses on design and fabrication of nanostructural optical sensors for disease diagnostics. From 2019–2020, she was involved in lab-on-a-chip-research and the development of molecular diagnostic techniques at University of New South Wales, Sydney. In 2020, she became a group leader of Carbon-based Optical Nanodevices for Sensors at the School of Optometry of Wenzhou Medical University.
